description: Standard: raw interview with a climate activist

input.transcript:
Q: What made you decide to start this organization?

A: So, um, I think — well, it's actually a really long story, but basically I was — I was working in, you know, corporate sustainability consulting, which is — it's kind of a joke, honestly, like, the things we were doing were just, they were just greenwashing, and I — I mean, I knew that at the time, I think everyone knows that, but, um, I had this moment where I was writing a report for a — for a major, you know, I can't say who — a major energy company, and the report was basically saying they were on track for net zero, and they were not. Like, not even close. And I just thought, I can't — I can't keep doing this.

Q: And the organization focuses specifically on holding companies accountable for their climate pledges?

A: Yeah, exactly. So what we do is, we — we take the pledges that companies make, the public pledges, net zero by 2040 or whatever it is, and we just — we track them. We track the actual emissions data against what they've promised. And, um, it's — the gap is enormous. Like, 80 percent of the companies in our database are not on track. Eighty percent. And these are not — these are not small companies, these are the ones making the biggest, loudest promises.

Q: What's next for the project?

A: Well, um, we're — we're basically hoping to, you know, expand into — into three new cities by next year.

description: European: interview with EU trade negotiator

input.transcript:
Q: The EU-Mercosur trade deal has been under negotiation for over 20 years. Why has it taken so long?

A: Well, I mean, the — the honest answer is that agriculture is — is always the problem. And I don't just mean EU agriculture, I mean — look, the French farmers don't want Brazilian beef competing at lower prices, and the Brazilian, um, the Brazilian soy producers don't want to comply with EU deforestation rules. So you have — you have these two immovable objects on either side, and the, you know, the trade negotiators are stuck in the middle trying to find creative ambiguity that satisfies both. And creative ambiguity has a — has a shelf life. At some point you actually have to decide what the rules are.

Q: Will the deal get done?

A: I think — honestly, I think it gets done in some form but it won't be the deal that anyone wanted. It'll be a deal of carve-outs and exceptions and phase-in periods that stretches over 15, maybe 20 years. Which is — I mean, that's not really a trade deal, that's a — that's a gentleman's agreement with a timeline.

description: African: politician interview with code-switching between English and local references

input.transcript:
Q: You've been in parliament for 12 years now. What has changed about how governance works in this country?

A: Ah, you see, when I first came in, the — the budget process was, let me tell you, it was like — you know how they say, the goat eats where it is tethered? That was the budget. Every minister was just, you know, eating where they were tethered. There was no — no central coordination. And the donors, the World Bank, IMF, they would come and say, you people must reform, you must have fiscal discipline, and everyone would nod, nod, nod, and then go back and do the same thing.

Q: And now?

A: Now it's — it's more sophisticated, I would say. The corruption is more sophisticated. I'm being honest with you. Before, it was crude. Somebody would just, you know, take the money and build a house. Now it's — it's public-private partnerships that somehow always benefit the same families. It's consultancy fees for reports that nobody reads. It's — what do they call it — capacity building workshops at five-star hotels. The money still goes, but it goes through proper channels now. Very proper.

Q: That's a remarkably candid assessment from a sitting member of parliament.

A: Well, what are they going to do? I'm in my last term. And besides, everybody knows. The people in the market know, the mama selling tomatoes knows, the boda-boda rider knows. The only people who pretend not to know are the ones in the system.

description: Adversarial: very poor audio quality transcript with gaps and inaudible sections

input.transcript:
Q: Can you walk us through what happened on the night of the [INAUDIBLE]?

A: So we were — [CROSSTALK] — sorry, go ahead — no, so we were at the — at the facility, the water treatment facility, and around, I think it was 11, maybe 11:30, [INAUDIBLE] — the alarm went off. The — [STATIC, 4 SECONDS] — and that's when we realized the valve had been [INAUDIBLE]. My colleague, he — [BACKGROUND NOISE] — he tried to call the supervisor but there was no [INAUDIBLE] signal, no cell signal out there.

Q: [INAUDIBLE] — the report says the chemical levels were —

A: Yeah, the — [GARBLED] — parts per million, which is — I mean, that's [INAUDIBLE] times the safe limit. And nobody — [SPEAKER OVERLAP] — nobody told the residents for, what, three days? Four days? The company says [INAUDIBLE] but I was there and I can tell you that's not — that is not what happened.

Q: What do you think should happen now?

A: [LONG PAUSE] I think — [SIGHS] — I think people need to [INAUDIBLE] — accountable. Someone needs to be held accountable. Not a — not a fine, not a — [INAUDIBLE] — a real, a real consequence.

description: East Asian: translated interview with cultural nuances and indirect speech patterns

input.transcript:
Q: Your company has grown from three employees to over 400 in six years. What is the secret?

A: I would not say there is a secret. Perhaps I have been fortunate. In our culture, we say that the nail that sticks up gets hammered down, so I was always careful not to appear too ambitious. When we started, I told my team, let us not try to be the best, let us try to be the most reliable. Reliability is — it is not glamorous, but customers remember it. My mentor, who I respect very deeply, he once told me, the company that survives is the one that makes itself necessary, not the one that makes itself famous. I think about that every day.

Q: You've been reluctant to expand internationally. Why?

A: That is a fair question. I think — how should I say — there are things that work here because of the relationships we have built over many years. These relationships are not transferable. If I open an office in another country, I am starting from zero in terms of trust. And trust is — well, without trust, you have only a contract, and a contract is a very cold thing to build a business on. So we are cautious. Some investors are frustrated with us, I know this. But I would rather be slow and respected than fast and regretted.

Q: Is there pressure from your board to grow faster?

A: [Laughs softly] The board has their perspective, and I have mine. We discuss. In the end, I think we understand each other. I would not want to say more than that.

description: Long-form: 2-hour deep dive interview needing heavy condensation

input.transcript:
Q: Let's start at the very beginning. You grew up in a small town. What was that like?

A: Yeah, so, um, it was — I mean, it was a town of maybe 8,000 people, very — very isolated, three hours from the nearest city, and my parents were both teachers, so we were — we were the educated family in town, which is — it's a weird position, you know, because people respected my parents but also kind of resented them, like, oh, they think they're better than us, which — they didn't, they absolutely didn't, but that was the perception.

Q: And that shaped your interest in education policy?

A: Not directly, no. I mean, I went to university, studied economics, worked in banking for — God — for almost 15 years. Made good money. Hated it. Absolutely hated it. And then my mother got sick, and I went back to take care of her, and I saw that the school where she'd taught for 30 years was — it was falling apart. Literally. The roof leaked, they had 45 kids in a classroom, one textbook for every six students. And this was not some remote village, this was — this was a town with a functioning government, taxes being collected, budgets being passed. The money was just going somewhere else.

Q: So you left banking.

A: I left banking. Everyone thought I was insane. My wife — she supported me, but I could tell she was terrified. We had a mortgage, two kids in school. But I just — I couldn't unsee it. Once you see that the system is broken and you understand how money works, you can't just go back to your desk and pretend.

Q: Walk me through what you did next.

A: OK so the first thing I did was — and this is going to sound really boring — I did a forensic audit of the district education budget. Three months, just me, a laptop, and the public records. And what I found was — I mean, it was not dramatic corruption, it was bureaucratic leakage. The procurement process was designed so that textbooks cost three times what they cost on the open market. The maintenance contracts went to one company, always the same company, owned by — well, I won't say, but connected. Teacher training budgets were spent on conferences that nobody attended. It was death by a thousand cuts.

Q: You published this analysis.

A: I published it online, just a blog post, really. Expected maybe 50 people to read it. It got picked up by a national outlet and suddenly I'm on television explaining school budget forensics, which is — I mean, I'm not a television person, I'm a spreadsheet person.

Q: That led to the foundation.

A: Eventually, yes. But first there were — there were two years of just consulting, going district to district, and what was depressing is that the pattern was the same everywhere. The numbers were different, the names were different, but the structure of the waste was identical. So we formalized it. Built a methodology. Trained local auditors. And now we've done this in — I think we're at 340 districts across six countries.

Q: What's the impact been?

A: We calculate that the reforms we've helped implement have redirected about $180 million back into actual classroom spending over the past four years. Which sounds like a lot, but when you spread it across 340 districts and six countries, it's — it's a start. The real impact is that local communities now have the tools to hold their own governments accountable. That's the sustainable part. I won't be around forever, but the methodology will be.

Q: Critics say you're imposing external frameworks on local systems.

A: Yeah, I've heard that. And I — I take it seriously. But the methodology is not prescriptive. We don't say, spend the money on X. We say, here's where the money is going, here's where it's supposed to go, here's the gap. What you do about the gap is your decision. If that's imposing an external framework, then arithmetic is an external framework.
